_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against 2022 AASLD hemochromatosis guideline, UpToDate, and Iron Disorders Institute resources. Ferritin and transferrin saturation thresholds verified against AASLD/AIC recommendations.

h1Title: Hereditary Hemochromatosis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Hereditary hemochromatosis is an autosomal recessive iron overload disorder caused by mutations in the HFE gene — most commonly C282Y homozygosity, found in approximately 1 in 200–400 individuals of Northern European descent. Excess iron deposits progressively in the liver (causing cirrhosis), pancreas (causing diabetes), heart (causing cardiomyopathy), joints (causing arthritis), and skin (causing hyperpigmentation), with the liver bearing the greatest burden of disease. The condition is diagnosed by elevated serum ferritin and transferrin saturation above 45 percent, confirmed by HFE genetic testing. Treatment by regular phlebotomy restores normal life expectancy when initiated before cirrhosis develops.

keyStats.prevalence: 1 in 200–400 people of Northern European descent carry C282Y homozygosity; penetrance (clinical disease) is approximately 25–50% in men and 10–25% in women
keyStats.demographics: 90% of cases in people of Northern European ancestry; symptoms typically appear in men aged 40–60 and post-menopausal women
keyStats.avgAge: Clinical manifestations usually appear after age 40 in men; women present 10–15 years later due to iron loss through menstruation
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 1 in 200–400 individuals of European descent are homozygous for C282Y

definition: Hereditary hemochromatosis (ICD-10: E83.1) is an autosomal recessive disorder of iron metabolism leading to excessive intestinal iron absorption and progressive iron accumulation in organs and tissues. The HFE protein normally regulates iron absorption by interacting with transferrin receptor 1 on enterocytes; mutations — most commonly C282Y (a cysteine-to-tyrosine substitution at position 282) and less frequently H63D — disrupt this regulatory interaction, causing unregulated iron uptake from the gut. Iron accumulates first in the liver (hepatocytes), then spreads to the pancreas, heart, joints, pituitary, and skin over decades. The liver bears the highest iron burden and is the first organ to show clinical injury — fibrosis, cirrhosis, and eventually hepatocellular carcinoma, which is the major cause of death in untreated patients. The disease follows a predictable staging model: genetic susceptibility plus elevated iron stores, progressing to tissue injury, and finally to clinical disease affecting specific organs. Penetrance is incomplete and sex-modified — estrogen-mediated iron loss in premenopausal women explains why women develop disease a decade or more later than men on average.

typesClassification.description: C282Y homozygosity — the most common form, responsible for roughly 80–90% of clinical cases in populations of Northern European descent. Presents with the classic triad of liver disease, diabetes, and skin bronzing.

Mutation in the hepcidin gene (HAMP) on chromosome 19, causing severe iron overload in children and young adults under 30 — faster progression than HFE-related disease with prominent cardiac and endocrine involvement.

Mutation in hemojuvelin (HJV), a co-receptor for bone morphogenetic proteins involved in hepcidin regulation. Produces the same juvenile phenotype as HAMP mutations, with severe iron overload before age 30.

Mutation in TFR2, a protein expressed on hepatocytes that normally senses iron levels and regulates hepcidin production. Causes moderate iron overload with hepatic and endocrine involvement in mid-adulthood.

Mutation in ferroportin, the iron export protein on enterocytes and macrophages. Two forms: loss-of-function (autosomal dominant iron accumulation primarily in macrophages, lower ferritin, no transferrin saturation rise) and gain-of-function (hepcidin-resistant, similar to HFE disease phenotype).

Includes mutations in genes beyond HFE — such as transferrin receptor 2, ferroportin, hepcidin, and hemojuvelin — that cause iron overload without the classic C282Y genotype. Account for approximately 5–10% of iron overload cases.

primarySymptoms: Fatigue and generalized weakness, often the earliest and most common presenting symptom — reported by 75–85% of symptomatic patients.
Joint pain and arthritis, most commonly affecting the second and third metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally (MCP 2 and 3) and the wrists, mimicking pseudogout — present in 20–50% of patients.

Bronze or slate-grey skin discoloration, most visible on the face, neck, and extensor surfaces — caused by iron and melanin deposition in the dermis, giving a characteristic appearance that historically led to the term 'bronze diabetes.'

Abdominal pain or hepatomegaly (enlarged liver) — the liver is the primary site of iron deposition, and patients may develop right upper quadrant discomfort, palpable liver edge, or frank liver disease.

Loss of libido, erectile dysfunction, or menstrual irregularities — caused by iron deposition in the pituitary gland suppressing gonadotropin-releasing hormone and leading to hypogonadotropic hypogonadism.
Type 2 diabetes mellitus from iron deposition in pancreatic beta cells, causing insulin deficiency — historically termed 'bronze diabetes' when combined with skin changes.

Cardiomyopathy with exertional dyspnea, reduced exercise tolerance, or heart failure — restrictive or dilated cardiomyopathy from myocardial iron deposition; cardiac involvement is the most common cause of death in untreated juvenile hemochromatosis.
Hypothyroidism from pituitary iron deposition causing secondary thyroid hormone deficiency — weight gain, cold intolerance, and fatigue.
Ascites, jaundice, or upper gastrointestinal bleeding from portal hypertension in patients who have progressed to cirrhosis — indicating advanced disease.
Spider nevi, palmar erythema, and other signs of chronic liver disease in patients with established cirrhosis.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained elevation in serum ferritin or transferrin saturation above 45% on routine blood work, even without symptoms
Family history of hemochromatosis or unexplained liver cirrhosis in a first-degree relative
Joint pain specifically affecting the second and third MCP joints bilaterally — a distinctive pattern that is otherwise uncommon
Unexplained diabetes or insulin resistance in a patient under 50, especially in someone of Northern European ancestry
Fatigue and loss of libido in a middle-aged man with elevated liver enzymes — the combination is suggestive enough to warrant iron studies
emergencySigns: Acute onset of confusion, drowsiness, or asterixis (liver flap) — possible hepatic encephalopathy from decompensated cirrhosis
Sudden upper gastrointestinal bleeding from esophageal varices — a life-threatening complication of portal hypertension in cirrhosis
Severe abdominal pain with ascites, fever, and elevated white count — possible spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, requiring urgent paracentesis
New onset of rapid irregular heartbeat or acute heart failure symptoms — iron-induced cardiac arrhythmia or decompensated cardiomyopathy
Deep jaundice (bilirubin >3 mg/dL) with coagulopathy (INR >1.5) — possible acute-on-chronic liver failure

causes.description: The single most common cause of hereditary hemochromatosis — approximately 80–90% of clinical cases in populations of Northern European descent. Two copies of the C282Y variant disrupt HFE protein function, preventing proper regulation of iron absorption through the hepcidin pathway.

A less common HFE genotype associated with moderate iron overload — present in approximately 5–10% of patients with iron overload. Penetrance is lower than C282Y homozygosity, and clinical disease usually less severe.

Biallelic mutations in hemojuvelin or hepcidin genes cause severe iron overload before age 30, primarily through hepcidin deficiency. Hepcidin is the key negative regulator of iron absorption — without it, iron flows unchecked from the gut into circulation and deposits in organs.

Rare autosomal recessive mutations in TFR2 cause iron overload through impaired sensing of plasma iron levels and inadequate hepcidin response. Causes a phenotype intermediate between classic HFE disease and juvenile forms.

Autosomal dominant mutations in the ferroportin iron export protein cause two distinct clinical patterns: the classic iron overload phenotype (gain-of-function with hepcidin resistance) or a macrophage-dominant iron storage pattern with lower transferrin saturation.

Repeated blood transfusions (e.g., in thalassemia or sickle cell disease) deliver large iron loads directly to the bloodstream; unlike HFE disease, this is not driven by increased gut absorption but by transfusion burden. Requires iron chelation therapy rather than phlebotomy.

Disorders with high red cell turnover and ineffective erythropoiesis (certain thalassemias, myelodysplastic syndromes) drive iron accumulation through both increased gut absorption (mediated by suppressed hepcidin) and transfusion dependence.

riskFactors.description: C282Y homozygosity frequency is highest in Northern European populations — approximately 1 in 200–400 in the UK, Ireland, Scandinavia, and their diaspora. The variant is rare or absent in East Asian, African, and South Asian populations.

Men develop clinical hemochromatosis 2–5 times more frequently than women at a given iron load, largely because women lose iron through menstruation, pregnancy, and lactation throughout their reproductive years.
Iron accumulates over decades before reaching the organ injury threshold; clinical disease manifests most often after age 40 in men and after menopause in women.

Alcohol synergizes with iron to accelerate hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis. Patients with hemochromatosis who consume more than 60 g of alcohol daily (roughly 4–5 standard drinks) have a dramatically elevated risk of cirrhosis.

Concurrent viral hepatitis dramatically accelerates progression to cirrhosis in hemochromatosis patients — the combination of iron overload and viral hepatitis is multiplicative in causing liver injury.

Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and iron overload share pathogenic overlap; NAFLD patients with elevated ferritin have worse fibrosis scores, and iron may worsen metabolic liver disease progression.

riskFactors.factor: High iron diet (fortified cereals, red meat, iron supplements)
riskFactors.description: Excessive iron intake — particularly from supplements or iron-fortified foods — accelerates iron loading in patients with the C282Y genotype, though dietary iron is a smaller contributor than genetic susceptibility.

High-dose vitamin C (above 500 mg daily) enhances non-heme iron absorption in the gut and can mobilize iron from stores, potentially increasing oxidative stress in iron-loaded organs. Patients with hemochromatosis should avoid high-dose vitamin C supplements.

Iron deposition in the pancreas directly causes beta-cell failure and diabetes — poorly controlled diabetes accelerates organ damage, while diabetes from hemochromatosis improves substantially with iron removal.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of hemochromatosis or unexplained cirrhosis
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of an affected individual should undergo HFE genetic testing and iron studies, as approximately 25% of siblings of a C282Y homozygote are also homozygous.

affectedDemographics: C282Y homozygosity prevalence is highest in Ireland (1 in 83), followed by Norway (1 in 134), Sweden (1 in 200), France (1 in 300), and the United States (1 in 300–400 for people of European descent)
African, East Asian, and South Asian populations carry very low C282Y prevalence but may have non-HFE iron overload disorders
Juvenile hemochromatosis (HJV/HAMP mutations) occurs worldwide regardless of ancestry and typically presents before age 30
Women represent roughly 30% of clinically symptomatic hemochromatosis cases, with symptoms usually appearing 10–15 years after menopause
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of hereditary hemochromatosis follows a stepwise approach that begins with iron studies, proceeds to genetic testing, and may require liver biopsy in selected cases. The first-line screening test is a combination of serum ferritin and transferrin saturation (calculated as serum iron divided by total iron-binding capacity, expressed as a percentage). Transferrin saturation above 45% is the most sensitive single marker for HFE hemochromatosis; ferritin is elevated in many conditions and less specific. In a patient of Northern European ancestry with transferrin saturation above 45%, the next step is HFE gene testing for C282Y and H63D variants. C282Y homozygosity confirms the diagnosis and no further testing is needed in most patients. Liver biopsy to quantitate hepatic iron concentration and assess fibrosis is reserved for patients with ferritin above 1000 ng/mL, elevated liver enzymes, or clinical signs of liver disease — it is not required in uncomplicated early cases. The hepatic iron index (hepatic iron concentration divided by age) greater than 1.9 supports the diagnosis. MRI with iron-sensitive sequences can non-invasively quantify liver and cardiac iron content and is increasingly used instead of biopsy in many centers. Once the diagnosis is established, first-degree relatives should be offered HFE testing and iron studies. The American College of Gastroenterology and AASLD recommend against population screening for HFE mutations in the general population — targeted testing in at-risk individuals (Northern European ancestry, unexplained elevated ferritin, family history) is the current standard.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most sensitive initial screening test for HFE hemochromatosis. Values above 45% (or two separate fasting measurements above 40%) are suggestive. Combined with ferritin, it identifies the majority of C282Y homozygotes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw after an overnight fast. Results typically available within 1–2 days. Transferrin saturation fluctuates more than ferritin — at least two elevated results are required before proceeding to genetic testing.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reflects total body iron stores. Elevated in iron overload but also in inflammation, liver disease, and malignancy — not specific for hemochromatosis. Guides treatment intensity and indicates when liver biopsy is needed (ferritin >1000 ng/mL with abnormal LFTs).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results same day or next day. Ferritin levels fall into the normal range with successful phlebotomy treatment and are monitored serially to track therapy progress.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive genetic test confirming or excluding HFE-related hemochromatosis. Identifies homozygous (C282Y/C282Y), compound heterozygous (C282Y/H63D), and heterozygous states. A positive test in a patient with elevated iron studies confirms the diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw or cheek swab sent to a molecular genetics lab. Results typically take 1–2 weeks. The test identifies the common HFE variants — if negative with strong clinical suspicion, broader gene panels or non-HFE hemochromatosis should be considered.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver biopsy with hepatic iron quantification
diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for assessing hepatic iron burden and fibrosis stage in patients with ferritin above 1000 ng/mL, elevated liver enzymes, or clinical signs of liver disease. Determines whether cirrhosis has already developed, which affects prognosis and monitoring.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A needle biopsy through the right lower chest wall under local anesthetic, lasting 15–20 minutes. The sample is stained for iron (Prussian blue) and scored, and iron concentration is measured. Risks include pain, bleeding (rare), and pneumothorax (very rare).

diagnosticTests.test: MRI with iron-sensitive sequences (R2*, quantitative susceptibility mapping)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive quantification of liver and cardiac iron content, increasingly used instead of liver biopsy for monitoring treatment response and detecting cardiac iron overload before cardiomyopathy develops.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard MRI of the abdomen and heart, taking 30–60 minutes. No radiation. The scan measures the rate of signal decay caused by iron deposition and produces a quantitative iron concentration estimate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses pancreatic beta-cell function and screens for diabetes mellitus, a common complication of hemochromatosis from iron deposition in the pancreas. Baseline diabetes assessment is essential before treatment planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw after overnight fast. HbA1c provides a 3-month average glucose picture; elevated HbA1c indicates established diabetes that may partially reverse with iron removal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for iron-induced cardiomyopathy in patients with elevated ferritin, cardiac symptoms, or juvenile hemochromatosis. Early detection allows treatment before irreversible cardiac damage occurs.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A non-invasive ultrasound of the heart lasting 30–45 minutes. Measures ejection fraction, wall thickness, and diastolic function — all of which may be impaired by iron deposition.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of hereditary hemochromatosis has two components: iron removal to reverse organ damage, and surveillance for complications that have already developed. The primary and most effective treatment is therapeutic phlebotomy — weekly removal of 500 mL of blood (equivalent to 200–250 mg of iron) until ferritin falls to the target range of 50–100 ng/mL. For most patients with significant iron overload, this requires 50–100 phlebotomies over 1–2 years. Once iron stores are depleted, a maintenance schedule (typically every 3–4 months) keeps ferritin in the target range for life. Phlebotomy reverses fatigue, improves skin pigmentation, normalizes liver enzymes, and can halt or partially reverse early cardiomyopathy and diabetes — the earlier it starts, the more complete the reversal. Patients who have developed cirrhosis before treatment starts have a significantly worse prognosis; cirrhosis is not fully reversible. Liver transplant is the definitive treatment for end-stage hemochromatosis liver disease and is the only intervention that can cure both the cirrhosis and the iron overload. Patients with established cirrhosis require hepatocellular carcinoma surveillance (ultrasound every 6 months) given their elevated cancer risk. Iron chelation therapy (deferasirox, deferoxamine, deferiprone) is reserved for patients who cannot tolerate phlebotomy — primarily those with severe anemia, heart failure, or transfusion-dependent secondary iron overload. Dietary modifications (avoiding iron supplements, vitamin C excess, raw shellfish) are adjunctive but do not replace phlebotomy.

medicalTreatments.name: Therapeutic phlebotomy (500 mL weekly)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for HFE hemochromatosis — removal of 500 mL of blood (approximately 200–250 mg iron) weekly until ferritin falls to 50–100 ng/mL, then maintenance phlebotomy every 3–4 months. Effective in over 95% of patients. Contraindicated in anemia (hemoglobin below 11 g/dL in women, 13 g/dL in men) and severe cardiac disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalizes survival to that of the general population when initiated before cirrhosis develops. Reverses fatigue, skin changes, diabetes (in 50–75%), cardiomyopathy (when early), and arthropathy in most patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Deferasirox (Exjade, Jadenu) 20–40 mg/kg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Oral iron chelator for patients who cannot tolerate phlebotomy — primarily those with severe anemia, advanced cardiac disease, or secondary iron overload from transfusions. Monitoring liver and kidney function is required due to hepatotoxicity and nephrotoxicity risks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces liver iron concentration by approximately 30–50% over 12 months at standard doses. Less effective than phlebotomy for HFE disease but valuable for patients with contraindications.

medicalTreatments.name: Deferoxamine (Desferal) subcutaneous infusion
medicalTreatments.description: Subcutaneous or intravenous iron chelator used primarily for transfusion-related iron overload (thalassemia, sickle cell disease). Administered as a 8–12 hour nightly infusion, 5–7 nights per week. Less commonly used in HFE hemochromatosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective at reducing iron burden when adherence is maintained; practical challenges limit real-world effectiveness in chronic HFE disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Angiotensin receptor blockers (losartan)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive therapy for hemochromatosis patients with liver fibrosis — losartan has anti-fibrotic properties in animal models and modestly reduces fibrosis progression in NAFLD. Not primary treatment but may reduce fibrosis progression in patients with concurrent NAFLD.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest benefit in reducing fibrosis progression in chronic liver disease; considered in selected patients with fibrosis not fully reversible with phlebotomy alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Pioglitazone for metabolic dysfunction
medicalTreatments.description: Thiazolidinedione insulin sensitizer used in some hemochromatosis patients with insulin resistance and NAFLD. Improves hepatic insulin sensitivity and can reduce liver fat in metabolic liver disease. Used off-label in selected patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces hepatic steatosis and insulin resistance in metabolic liver disease; the benefit in iron-related liver disease is modest.

medicalTreatments.name: Erythrocytapheresis
medicalTreatments.description: An apheresis technique that selectively removes red blood cells (iron-rich) while returning plasma to the patient — allows removal of more iron per session than standard phlebotomy with less hemodynamic stress. Used in specialized centers.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Removes roughly twice the iron per session compared to standard phlebotomy; useful in patients who cannot tolerate large volume blood loss, such as those with cardiac disease.

surgicalOptions.name: Liver transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: The definitive treatment for end-stage hemochromatosis cirrhosis. Transplant cures both the cirrhosis and the iron overload (the transplanted liver has normal HFE regulation). Survival after transplant for hemochromatosis is similar to other indications but cardiac evaluation pre-transplant is critical due to potential cardiac iron.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year survival after liver transplant for hemochromatosis is approximately 70–80% in current eras. Cardiac iron is a major predictor of post-transplant mortality — cardiac MRI to assess cardiac iron before listing is essential.

surgicalOptions.name: Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT)
surgicalOptions.description: Pacemaker-based treatment for iron-induced cardiomyopathy with severe left ventricular dysfunction and electrical dyssynchrony. Not a cure for iron overload but addresses the cardiomyopathy complication in patients who have progressed to heart failure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Beneficial in iron-loaded cardiomyopathy when standard heart failure criteria are met; cardiac iron removal must accompany device placement to prevent progressive deterioration.

alternativeTreatments.name: Green tea extract (EGCG)
alternativeTreatments.description: Polyphenols in green tea have modest iron-chelating properties in vitro and may reduce intestinal iron absorption. Not a substitute for phlebotomy but potentially useful as an adjunct in early-stage disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Turmeric (curcumin)
alternativeTreatments.description: Animal studies suggest curcumin may reduce hepatic iron accumulation and fibrosis through antioxidant and anti-inflammatory mechanisms. No robust human trial data; not a substitute for established therapy.

whySeeSpecialist: A hepatologist or gastroenterologist should manage hemochromatosis in patients with elevated ferritin, liver enzyme abnormalities, or cirrhosis, in coordination with a hematologist for phlebotomy management. An endocrinologist is appropriate for diabetes and hypogonadism management. A cardiologist should evaluate patients with cardiac symptoms or ferritin above 1000 ng/mL. Genetic counseling is recommended for patients and their family members.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a hepatologist or gastroenterologist experienced in iron overload disorders. Ask whether they use transferrin saturation and ferritin as first-line screening, offer HFE genetic testing, and have a structured phlebotomy protocol. Centers specializing in hemoglobinopathies or hematology often have the most experience with iron overload. For family screening, a clinical geneticist can coordinate cascade testing.
preventionStrategies: First-degree relatives of a patient with confirmed HFE hemochromatosis should undergo HFE genetic testing and iron studies, regardless of symptoms — approximately 25% of siblings will be affected
Avoid iron supplements, high-dose vitamin C (above 500 mg daily), and excessive red meat intake — modest contributors to iron loading in susceptible individuals
Limit alcohol to under 1 standard drink daily — alcohol synergizes with iron to accelerate hepatic fibrosis and cirrhosis in hemochromatosis
Maintain a healthy weight and manage NAFLD aggressively if present — metabolic liver disease compounds iron-related liver injury
Screen for and treat hepatitis B and C co-infection — viral hepatitis dramatically accelerates cirrhosis in iron-loaded livers
lifestyleModifications: Donate blood regularly once hemochromatosis is diagnosed and under treatment — therapeutic blood donation is as effective as physician-administered phlebotomy for many patients, and donated blood can help others
Avoid raw or undercooked shellfish — patients with liver disease are at increased risk of Vibrio vulnificus infection, which thrives in warm seawater and can cause fatal sepsis in cirrhotic patients
Limit alcohol to under 1 drink daily (2 for men, 1 for women) — alcohol potentiates hepatic iron toxicity and accelerates progression to cirrhosis
Avoid iron-fortified breakfast cereals, red meat in excess, and iron supplements — these contribute modestly to iron loading on top of the genetic tendency
Maintain up-to-date vaccinations against hepatitis A and B — chronic hepatitis co-infection dramatically worsens outcomes in hemochromatosis
Exercise regularly but avoid extreme endurance activities that dramatically increase iron需求 through sweat losses — moderate aerobic exercise is beneficial for cardiovascular and metabolic health

recommended: Tea (black or green) with meals — tannins reduce non-heme iron absorption by up to 50%; drinking tea with iron-rich meals is one of the most evidence-based dietary interventions
Calcium-rich foods with iron-rich meals — calcium inhibits iron absorption; dairy products, calcium supplements, or calcium-fortified foods consumed simultaneously with iron sources reduce absorption
High-phytate foods (whole grains, legumes, nuts) — phytates are potent iron absorption inhibitors; encourages consumption of whole plant foods rather than fortified grains
Coffee — coffee consumption is associated with modestly reduced iron absorption; drinking coffee with meals can reduce the iron dose from food
Lean white fish and poultry in moderation — lower iron density than red meat; protein source without the iron load of beef, pork, or lamb
avoid: Iron supplements (unless prescribed for documented iron deficiency) — even low-dose multivitamins with iron can contribute to iron loading in HFE homozygotes
High-dose vitamin C supplements — vitamin C strongly enhances non-heme iron absorption and can mobilize iron from stores, increasing oxidative stress in organs
Raw or undercooked shellfish — cirrhotic patients are at risk for Vibrio vulnificus sepsis from raw oysters and clams; always cook shellfish thoroughly
Red meat in excess — beef, pork, lamb, and organ meats are heme iron sources with much higher bioavailability than plant iron; limit to 1–2 times per week
Fortified breakfast cereals and meal replacement bars — iron fortification is widespread and adds to dietary iron load in people with hemochromatosis
Alcohol above 1–2 standard drinks daily — alcohol accelerates hepatic fibrosis progression in iron-loaded livers

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise (150 minutes per week of walking, cycling, or swimming) is safe and beneficial in hemochromatosis patients without advanced cardiomyopathy. Exercise improves insulin sensitivity, cardiovascular health, and fatigue. Avoid extreme endurance events (ultramarathons, ironman distance triathlons) due to significant iron losses through sweat and GI blood loss, which can mask the need for phlebotomy adjustments. In patients with established cardiomyopathy, exercise prescription should be individualized by a cardiologist.
dailyManagement: Schedule and keep regular phlebotomy appointments — adherence is the single most important factor in treatment success
Monitor ferritin every 3 months during the iron depletion phase and every 6–12 months in the maintenance phase
Check complete blood count before each phlebotomy to ensure hemoglobin is adequate to tolerate blood removal
Vaccinate against hepatitis A and B if not already immune — co-infection dramatically worsens liver outcomes
Avoid raw shellfish and ensure all fish and shellfish is thoroughly cooked — cirrhotic patients are at risk for serious bacterial infection
Inform all clinicians (including dentists) of the hemochromatosis diagnosis and the need to avoid iron supplements — this prevents inadvertent iatrogenic iron loading
prognosis: The prognosis of hereditary hemochromatosis is fundamentally determined by the stage at which treatment begins. Patients diagnosed and treated with phlebotomy before cirrhosis develops have a normal life expectancy — iron removal prevents further organ damage and reverses most established complications (diabetes, cardiomyopathy, arthropathy, skin changes). Liver enzymes normalize, fibrosis can regress, and quality of life improves dramatically with treatment. Once cirrhosis has developed before treatment starts, the prognosis diverges significantly: patients with established cirrhosis carry an approximately 200-fold increased risk of hepatocellular carcinoma, require lifelong surveillance with imaging every 6 months, and have reduced survival even after iron depletion. Liver transplant can rescue patients with end-stage cirrhosis and provides a cure, with 5-year survival of approximately 70–80%. The leading causes of death in treated hemochromatosis are hepatocellular carcinoma (30–45% of deaths in cirrhotic patients) and cirrhosis complications. The key message is that early detection — before symptoms or cirrhosis appear — transforms the prognosis from a serious disease to a fully manageable one.
recoveryTimeline: Ferritin typically falls by 30–50 ng/mL per phlebotomy session in the depletion phase. Most patients achieve target ferritin (50–100 ng/mL) within 12–24 months of weekly phlebotomy (50–100 sessions total), after which maintenance phlebotomy every 3–4 months is required indefinitely. Symptom improvement is gradual: fatigue often improves within 3 months, skin bronzing fades over 1–2 years, diabetes and cardiomyopathy improve substantially over 12–24 months in patients without advanced organ damage. Arthropathy is the least reversible complication — joint symptoms often stabilize but may not fully resolve even with iron depletion. Cirrhosis, once established, is not reversible, though iron removal stops further progression and reduces HCC risk.
complications: Hepatocellular carcinoma — the most serious complication of hemochromatosis cirrhosis; risk is approximately 200-fold elevated compared to the general population; occurs even after iron depletion in cirrhotic patients; requires surveillance imaging every 6 months
Cirrhosis and portal hypertension — irreversible scarring of the liver from decades of iron overload; leads to ascites, esophageal varices, hepatic encephalopathy, and liver failure

Diabetes mellitus (bronze diabetes) — iron deposition in pancreatic beta cells causes insulin deficiency; partial or complete reversal is possible with early iron removal but established diabetes is often permanent

Cardiomyopathy and heart failure — iron infiltration of the myocardium causes restrictive or dilated cardiomyopathy; early treatment can reverse dysfunction, but advanced cardiac iron causes irreversible damage and is the leading cause of death in juvenile hemochromatosis

Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism — iron deposition in the anterior pituitary suppresses gonadotropin secretion, causing testosterone deficiency in men and hypogonadism in both sexes; may not fully reverse with iron removal but testosterone replacement improves symptoms

supportResources.name: Iron Disorders Institute — Hemochromatosis
supportResources.description: Patient-focused education on iron overload disorders, including a hemochromatosis diet guide and physician finder.

supportResources.name: American Hemochromatosis Society
supportResources.description: Advocacy organization providing genetic testing resources, physician education, and patient support.

supportResources.name: AASLD Hemochromatosis Guideline 2022
supportResources.description: Evidence-based clinical practice guideline on hemochromatosis from the American Association for the Study of Liver Diseases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: NAFLD causes elevated ferritin in 20–30% of patients through inflammation (ferritin is an acute-phase reactant), but transferrin saturation is usually normal or only mildly elevated, not above 45%. NAFLD does not cause the clinical triad of bronzed skin, diabetes, and hepatomegaly, and HFE genetic testing is negative.

confusedWithConditions.name: Alcoholic Liver Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Alcoholic liver disease causes elevated ferritin and can cause cirrhosis, but HFE genetic testing is negative, transferrin saturation is usually less elevated than in HFE hemochromatosis, and the skin bronzing and arthropathy pattern of hemochromatosis is absent.

confusedWithConditions.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Iron deficiency causes low ferritin (the body's iron store is depleted) and elevated transferrin (more unsaturated transferrin in circulation), the opposite pattern from hemochromatosis. Patients with iron deficiency feel fatigue, have low hemoglobin, and lack the skin and organ manifestations of hemochromatosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Hepatitis C
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hepatitis C can cause cirrhosis and elevated ferritin, but HFE genetic testing is negative, transferrin saturation is usually not elevated above 45%, and the systemic features of hemochromatosis (bronzing, diabetes, arthropathy) are absent. The two can co-exist, and iron overload worsens hepatitis C outcomes.

relatedConditions.name: Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: NAFLD and hemochromatosis frequently coexist, particularly in patients with metabolic syndrome; iron overload accelerates fibrosis progression in NAFLD, and both require management.

relatedConditions.name: Alcoholic Liver Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Alcohol consumption potentiates iron hepatotoxicity; the combination of HFE hemochromatosis and heavy alcohol use causes cirrhosis at a younger age and more severe fibrosis.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Hepatitis C
relatedConditions.relevance: Hepatitis C and iron overload synergistically worsen liver fibrosis; iron depletion improves treatment response in some hepatitis C patients; both should be treated when present.

relatedConditions.name: Cirrhosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The primary driver of morbidity and mortality in untreated hemochromatosis; iron accumulation in hepatocytes over decades causes fibrosis, cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
relatedConditions.relevance: Iron deposition in pancreatic beta cells causes insulin deficiency; diabetes in hemochromatosis often partially reverses with iron removal, distinguishing it from typical type 2 diabetes.

faqs.question: Is hemochromatosis curable?
faqs.answer: Hemochromatosis is not curable in the genetic sense — the HFE mutation persists for life. However, it is fully treatable. Phlebotomy removes excess iron, normalizes life expectancy when started before cirrhosis, and reverses most complications. The treatment is lifelong maintenance phlebotomy, similar to managing a chronic condition.

faqs.question: How is hemochromatosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The first step is a blood test for transferrin saturation and serum ferritin. Transferrin saturation above 45% (on two separate occasions) suggests iron overload. If these are elevated, HFE genetic testing confirms or excludes the most common form (C282Y homozygosity). Liver biopsy or MRI is reserved for patients with high ferritin or signs of liver disease.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for hemochromatosis?
faqs.answer: The primary treatment is therapeutic phlebotomy — removal of 500 mL of blood weekly until iron stores are depleted (ferritin falls to 50–100 ng/mL). This takes most patients 1–2 years with weekly sessions. Maintenance phlebotomy every 3–4 months is required for life. Patients who cannot tolerate phlebotomy use iron chelation drugs instead.

faqs.question: Is hemochromatosis genetic?
faqs.answer: Yes — hereditary hemochromatosis is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the HFE gene. C282Y homozygosity (two copies of the variant) is the most common cause. If one parent is a carrier, children have a 50% chance of being a carrier; if both parents are carriers, children have a 25% chance of being affected. First-degree relatives of an affected person should be tested.

faqs.question: What does high iron saturation mean?
faqs.answer: Transferrin saturation is the percentage of the iron-transport protein transferrin that is occupied by iron. A value above 45% suggests iron overload and warrants further testing with HFE genetic testing. Iron saturation can be temporarily elevated by inflammation, so two elevated readings are required before proceeding.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ferritin and iron saturation?
faqs.answer: Serum ferritin reflects the total amount of iron stored in the body — it is elevated in iron overload but also in inflammation, liver disease, and infection (since ferritin is also an acute-phase reactant). Transferrin saturation reflects how much iron is circulating in the blood at the time of the test — it is more specific for iron overload and is the best initial screening test.

faqs.question: Can you donate blood if you have hemochromatosis?
faqs.answer: Yes — in many countries including the United States, patients with hemochromatosis are allowed to donate blood for therapeutic use. Therapeutic blood donation is as effective as physician-administered phlebotomy, and the blood can be used for other patients, making it both treatment and community service. Check local blood bank regulations.

faqs.question: What foods should I avoid with hemochromatosis?
faqs.answer: Avoid iron supplements, high-dose vitamin C (above 500 mg daily), excessive red meat, and iron-fortified foods. Drink tea or coffee with meals — tannins and polyphenols significantly reduce non-heme iron absorption. Calcium-rich foods with iron-rich meals also inhibit iron absorption.

faqs.question: Does hemochromatosis cause diabetes?
faqs.answer: Yes — iron deposition in the pancreas damages beta cells, reducing insulin production and causing diabetes mellitus in 30–50% of patients with clinically significant iron overload. This is sometimes called 'bronze diabetes' when combined with skin bronzing. With early phlebotomy, diabetes can partially or fully reverse; established diabetes is harder to reverse.

faqs.question: What is bronze diabetes?
faqs.answer: Bronze diabetes is the historic term for the combination of skin bronzing (iron and melanin deposition in the skin) and diabetes mellitus in patients with advanced hemochromatosis. The name reflects the characteristic appearance: skin discoloration from iron plus glucose intolerance from pancreatic iron. It represents late-stage disease and is now less common with earlier detection.

faqs.question: Why are women protected from hemochromatosis?
faqs.answer: Premenopausal women lose iron through menstrual blood loss, pregnancy, and breastfeeding — approximately 500–1000 mg of iron per year on average through these routes. This iron loss delays the accumulation of iron burden that drives organ damage, explaining why women typically develop clinical hemochromatosis 10–15 years later than men, after menopause.

faqs.question: Can hemochromatosis cause heart problems?
faqs.answer: Yes — iron deposition in the heart muscle causes cardiomyopathy, most commonly a restrictive pattern early on that can progress to dilated cardiomyopathy and heart failure. Cardiac involvement is the leading cause of death in untreated juvenile hemochromatosis. Early phlebotomy can reverse cardiac dysfunction before irreversible damage occurs.

faqs.question: What joint problems does hemochromatosis cause?
faqs.answer: Hemochromatosis causes an arthritis that most commonly affects the second and third metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints of both hands bilaterally — a distinctive pattern. It also commonly affects the wrists, elbows, and knees. The arthritis can resemble pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate disease) due to calcium pyrophosphate crystal deposition in joints loaded with iron. Arthropathy is often the first symptom in younger patients.

faqs.question: Is hemochromatosis the same as iron deficiency?
faqs.answer: No — they are opposites. Iron deficiency means low body iron stores (low ferritin), causing fatigue and anemia. Hemochromatosis means excessive iron stores (high ferritin and high transferrin saturation), causing organ damage over decades. The treatments are opposite as well: iron deficiency is treated with iron supplements; hemochromatosis is treated with iron removal.

faqs.question: What is the hepatic iron index?
faqs.answer: The hepatic iron index is a calculation derived from liver biopsy: hepatic iron concentration (in micrograms per gram of dry weight) divided by the patient's age in years. A value above 1.9 is diagnostic of primary hemochromatosis. It is primarily used historically when genetic testing was not available — HFE testing has largely replaced this calculation in current practice.

faqs.question: Should family members be tested for hemochromatosis?
faqs.answer: Yes — first-degree relatives (siblings, children, parents) of a person with confirmed HFE hemochromatosis should undergo HFE genetic testing and iron studies. Approximately 25% of siblings of a C282Y homozygote are also homozygous. Testing allows early treatment before organ damage develops.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with hemochromatosis?
faqs.answer: Patients diagnosed and treated before cirrhosis develops have a normal life expectancy — identical to people without the condition. Once cirrhosis has developed before treatment starts, life expectancy is reduced due to complications including hepatocellular carcinoma, portal hypertension, and liver failure. Early detection through family screening or elevated iron studies in asymptomatic individuals is critical.

faqs.question: Does alcohol make hemochromatosis worse?
faqs.answer: Yes — heavy alcohol consumption (more than 2 drinks daily for women, 3 for men) synergizes with iron to dramatically accelerate liver fibrosis and cirrhosis in hemochromatosis patients. Patients with hemochromatosis should limit alcohol to 1 standard drink daily or fewer. Even moderate drinking significantly raises cirrhosis risk on top of iron overload.

faqs.question: Can hemochromatosis cause liver cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes — hepatocellular carcinoma is the most serious long-term complication of hemochromatosis, causing 30–45% of deaths in patients with established cirrhosis. The risk is approximately 200-fold higher than in the general population. Even after iron depletion with phlebotomy, once cirrhosis has developed, HCC risk remains elevated. Surveillance with ultrasound every 6 months is essential for all hemochromatosis patients with cirrhosis.

faqs.question: What is non-HFE hemochromatosis?
faqs.answer: Non-HFE hemochromatosis refers to iron overload disorders caused by mutations in genes other than HFE — including transferrin receptor 2 (TFR2), hepcidin (HAMP), hemojuvelin (HJV), and ferroportin (SLC40A1). These are less common than HFE hemochromatosis but may present in patients of non-Northern European ancestry or with clinical iron overload and negative HFE testing.
